title: How to Open a US Bank Account
summary: You should open a bank account within your first week in the US. This guide covers the process, required documents, and recommended banks.

content.text: Opening a bank account is one of the most important things to do after arriving in the US. With a bank account, you can safely store funds, receive transfers, pay rent, and handle daily expenses.
We recommend opening a bank account within your first week of arrival. The sooner you open one, the easier it is to manage your finances.

items: Phone number (if you have one already)

content.text: Some banks allow you to pre-open an account online before arrival. Check the bank's website for this option.

items.label: Chase
items.description: Most branches, great mobile app, free student account. Recommended first choice.

items.label: Bank of America
items.description: Many branches, partners with Bank of China for easy transfers.

items.label: Wells Fargo
items.description: Wide services, student account available. Mixed reviews recently.

items.label: Campus Credit Union
items.description: Lowest fees but limited locations. Great if you stay on campus.

items.title: Choose a bank
items.description: Consider the closest branch/ATM locations and student discounts.

items.title: Walk in or make an appointment
items.description: Most banks don't require appointments, but booking one reduces wait time.

items.title: Tell the teller you want a student account
items.description: Mention you're an international student — they'll guide you through everything.

items.title: Submit your documents
items.description: Present your passport, I-20, and admission letter.

items.title: Make an initial deposit
items.description: Usually need to deposit at least $25 to activate the account.

items.title: Set up online banking
items.description: Download the bank's app and set up login credentials and notifications.

content.text: Don't keep all your money in one place. We recommend opening both a Checking account (for daily spending) and a Savings account (for larger funds).

After opening your account, you'll receive a debit card for purchases and ATM withdrawals. Credit cards require a separate application — we suggest using your debit card for a few months first, then consider applying for a credit card.
Building credit history is important for future apartment rentals and phone plans. Consider applying for a student credit card or secured credit card to start building credit.

title: How to Transfer Money from China to the US
summary: Learn about different ways to transfer money, fee comparisons, and important tips.

content.text: After arriving in the US, you may need to transfer money from China to pay tuition and living expenses. Here are several common methods.

items.label: Bank Wire Transfer
items.description: Most traditional method. Transfer directly from a Chinese bank to your US account. Fees around $15-45, takes 1-3 business days.

items.label: Alipay/WeChat International
items.description: Convenient and fast, but lower per-transaction limits. Good for small amounts.

items.label: Wise (formerly TransferWise)
items.description: Great exchange rates, transparent fees. Recommended for large transfers.

items.label: Carrying Cash
items.description: You can bring up to $10,000 into the US without declaring. Amounts over $10,000 must be declared at customs.

content.text: China has an annual foreign exchange limit of $50,000 equivalent per person. Plan ahead if you need more, or pay tuition directly through school channels.
Consider transferring money in batches when exchange rates are favorable rather than one large transfer. Use a rate-tracking app to monitor rates.
